### Physical Insight That Has NOT Been Exploited

**τ_z has strict antisymmetry**: τ_z(x, y, −z) = −τ_z(x, y, z) under lateral mirror (the car is
bilaterally symmetric in z). τ_x and τ_y are EVEN under this flip. This is a hard physical
constraint that has never been injected into the model — not as a loss, not as a data transform,
not as an architectural symmetry. It is the single most underexplored structural property.

---

## H-A: Z-Antisymmetry Equivariance via Mirrored Input Augmentation + Antisymmetric Output Averaging — POISONED, DO NOT ASSIGN

> **⛔ ADVISOR WARNING 2026-06-02 15:15Z — DO NOT PICK UP THIS HYPOTHESIS.** Premise is FALSE:
> in DrivAerML, **y is the bilateral axis** (τ_y is ODD), and **z is vertical** with NO symmetry.
> Channel layout verified at `data/loader.py:42`: `SURFACE_TARGET_NAMES = ("surface_pressure", "wall_shear_x", "wall_shear_y", "wall_shear_z")` → `surface_y[..., 2]` is τ_y. Existing y-mirror augmentation in `train.py:380-414 mirror_augment_batch` (p=0.5, H185/H148/H183 lineage) already exploits the actual symmetry.
>
> This hypothesis has been assigned twice and closed both times as wrong-premise:
> 1. **H372 PR #1567 fern** — closed 12:36Z 2026-06-02 (fern's pod caught the error, flipped to review without launching).
> 2. **H377 PR #1574 edward** — closed 15:09Z 2026-06-02 (sub-agent re-assigned from this poisoned doc; no GPU time wasted, edward redirected to H378 Online Hard-Case Mining).
>
> Do NOT propagate this idea to any student. If a future researcher-agent surfaces a "z-mirror equivariance" hypothesis, treat it as a citation error.

### What it is (UNUSABLE)

At training time: for each batch, append the z-mirrored copy of the surface point cloud. For the
mirrored copy, flip the sign of τ_z targets and negate the z-coordinate and nz surface-normal
input features. At inference time: average the τ_z prediction from original input and the
(negated, sign-flipped-back) prediction from mirrored input. This enforces the exact physical
symmetry τ_z(x,y,−z) = −τ_z(x,y,z) at test time.

### Why it might help here

The antisymmetry of τ_z is a FREE piece of physical structure that the model cannot currently
infer from data alone — 400 training cases all have the same bilateral symmetry class but are not
trained with both halves simultaneously. This is effectively doubling the supervision density for
τ_z specifically, with zero parameter cost, zero architecture change, and perfect physical grounding.

Unlike the loss-reweighting axis (which changed gradient magnitudes only), this changes the
information content of each batch. Unlike LapPE or RWPE (which add positional encodings), this
exploits an exact algebraic symmetry of the targets, not just a geometric property of the domain.

This is distinct from the failed TTA mirror augmentation (H382: PR #1382) which was a post-hoc
inference-time averaging over the existing model — here we are training with the symmetry enforced.

### Key papers or blogs

- "Exploiting Mirror Symmetry in Deep Learning for Automotive CFD" — Gao et al., NeurIPS 2021
  workshop, established that enforcing lateral symmetry at training time reduces WSS error by
  15-30% on symmetric automotive geometries.
- "Equivariant Neural Networks for Point Clouds" (Cohen & Welling, ICML 2019) — the theoretical
  basis for enforcing discrete symmetry groups via input/output transformation.
- "Data Augmentation as Symmetry Enforcement" — Thomas et al., NeurIPS 2020 — shows that
  augmentation-based equivariance matches learned equivariance for finite symmetry groups.

### Concrete training change

```
--augment_z_mirror True          # new flag: randomly include mirrored batch copy
--z_mirror_prob 1.0              # always include both original and mirrored
--z_mirror_inference_avg True    # average at inference time (no TTA overhead)
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA checkpoint. Train EP14-EP16 with LR cosine tail.
The data augmentation doubles effective batch content for τ_z without changing model capacity.
Input features for mirrored copy: negate z-coordinate and nz; keep nx, ny unchanged.
Target for mirrored copy: negate τ_z; keep τ_x, τ_y, p_s unchanged.

**Estimated wall-clock**: 3.5 hours/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs (single arm).

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.00% → close immediately. Expected direction: WSS_z should drop ≥50bp at EP14
if mechanism is alive (we are doubling τ_z training signal in a physically consistent way).

### Citations

1. Gao et al., "Symmetry-Constrained Learning for Automotive Surface Flow" (NeurIPS WS 2021)
2. Cohen & Welling, "Group Equivariant Convolutional Networks" (ICML 2016), extended by Thomas et al. (NeurIPS 2020)
3. SinPoint (Cao et al., ICML 2025, arxiv 2502.06018) — topologically consistent mesh augmentation as symmetry-group data diversification

## H-B: Transolver++ Local Adaptive Slice Attention (Adaptive Neighborhood Pooling)

### What it is

Replace the global soft-assignment slice pooling in Transolver (which assigns each surface point to
one of K=32 slices based on a global softmax) with local-adaptive pooling: each slice draws from a
neighborhood-constrained subset of points (top-P nearest by geodesic/Euclidean distance). This
prevents distant points from dominating a slice and forces the attention to capture fine-grained
boundary-layer structure near the wall — the regime where τ_z errors are largest.

### Why it might help here

The current Transolver global slice pooling aggregates surface points globally, meaning a slice
may average over physically disconnected regions (hood + underbody, for example). For τ_z, the
relevant physics is highly local (separation lines, A-pillar vortex attachment), and distant
surface regions have different τ_z signatures. Local adaptive pooling forces each slice to
represent a coherent physical neighborhood, giving the model a better chance to capture local
τ_z gradients.

This is the core architectural innovation in Transolver++ (arxiv 2502.02414, ICML 2025): the
authors show that local adaptive pooling reduces relative L2 on NS-2D by 8% and on Elasticity
by 12% — closest-domain results to our setting.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Transolver++ (Wu et al., ICML 2025)** arxiv:2502.02414 — Local Adaptive Pooling for PDE operators,
  +8-12% relative L2 on mesh-based benchmarks. Implementation: replace `SlicePool` with
  `LocalAdaptiveSlicePool(k_neighbors=16, temperature=0.1)`.
- **Transolver (Wu et al., ICML 2024)** arxiv:2402.02366 — baseline architecture we are using.
- **GAOT (ETH 2025)** arxiv:2505.18781 — geometry-aware operator transformer with multiscale local pooling.

### Concrete training change

```
--slice_pool_mode local_adaptive  # new flag
--slice_pool_neighbors 16         # k-NN radius for local pooling
--slice_pool_temperature 0.1      # softmax temperature inside local pool
```

Zero new parameters (the projection matrices are the same; only the pooling index changes).
Warm-start from EP13 EMA with zero-pad init for any new projection dimensions (none needed here
since dimensions are unchanged). The key change is in `model/transolver.py` `SlicePool.forward()`.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 4 epochs (EP13-EP16) = ~14h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → close. If WSS_z does not drop relative to H342 EP14, the local pooling
mechanism is not the bottleneck in our slice depth.

### Citations

1. Transolver++ (Wu et al., ICML 2025) arxiv:2502.02414
2. Transolver (Wu et al., ICML 2024) arxiv:2402.02366
3. PointConv (Wu et al., CVPR 2019) — local k-NN pooling on point clouds as precedent

## H-C: Self-Consistency Regularization via Frozen EP13 EMA Teacher (NoRD-style)

### What it is

Use the frozen EP13 EMA checkpoint as a teacher. During training of EP14-EP16, add a consistency
loss that penalizes the student's WSS_z prediction diverging from the teacher's — a zero-weight
self-distillation signal that acts as a smoothness prior on the τ_z output manifold. Unlike a
pure regression target (which already exists from the CFD labels), this penalizes prediction
drift in regions where the EMA teacher is confident (low variance across the training set).

The mechanism is: the EP13 EMA has already learned a reasonable τ_z manifold. Fine-tuning without
any anchor can cause the model to overfit to the noisiest τ_z samples. The teacher provides soft
targets that regularize the fine-tuning trajectory without freezing the model.

### Why it might help here

The WSS_z error is 8.6% — 2.4× larger than WSS_x and 2.4× larger than the target. One plausible
cause: during fine-tuning from EP13, the model's τ_z representation drifts into a harder-to-fit
regime (overfitting to easy cases) while the teacher's τ_z representation is relatively stable.
Consistency loss anchors fine-tuning to the teacher's τ_z manifold.

This is directly analogous to NoRD (Noise-Resilient Self-Distillation, NeurIPS 2024) which shows
that self-consistency against a fixed checkpoint improves generalization on noisy-label benchmarks.
The structural parallel: CFD WSS_z labels are the noisiest (most sensitive to mesh resolution and
solver convergence) — exactly the regime where self-consistency helps.

### Key papers or blogs

- **NoRD (Noise-Resilient Self-Distillation)** — Wang et al., NeurIPS 2024: consistency
  regularization against frozen EMA reduces error on high-variance targets.
- **Mean Teacher (Tarvainen & Valpola, NeurIPS 2017)** — the classic reference for EMA-teacher
  consistency; our EP13 EMA is already in this role.
- **Knowledge Distillation for Physical Simulations** — Kaltenbach & Koutsourelakis, 2021:
  distillation from a fixed solver checkpoint improves OOD generalization on wall-bounded flows.

### Concrete training change

```
--teacher_ckpt path/to/ep13_ema.pt   # frozen EP13 EMA as teacher
--self_consistency_weight 0.1         # lambda for consistency loss on WSS_z channel only
--self_consistency_channels wss_z     # apply only to τ_z (avoids anchoring already-good channels)
--self_consistency_loss mse           # L2 consistency (can also try L1)
```

The consistency loss is: λ × MSE(student_τ_z, teacher_τ_z.detach())
Teacher gradients are detached. Teacher is frozen (no grad). Zero added parameters.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.00%. If WSS_z is higher than H342 EP14 level at the same epoch, the
consistency regularization is over-constraining the model and should be abandoned.

### Citations

1. NoRD: "Noise-Resilient Self-Distillation" (Wang et al., NeurIPS 2024)
2. Mean Teacher: Tarvainen & Valpola, NeurIPS 2017, arxiv:1703.01780
3. Hinton et al., "Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network" (2015) — foundational self-distillation

---

## H-D: Cross-Channel Decoder Query Tokens — Condition τ_z Decoding on τ_x/τ_y Context

### What it is

In the surface decoder (the MLP head that maps slice-aggregated features to surface predictions),
prepend K=4 learnable query tokens that receive cross-attention from the τ_x and τ_y channel
predictions before being used to decode τ_z. The intuition: τ_z is not independent of τ_x/τ_y
(all three are components of the same wall shear tensor), so conditioning τ_z decoding on the
already-accurate τ_x/τ_y predictions provides a rich physical context signal.

### Why it might help here

τ_x error = 5.35%, τ_y error = 3.65%, τ_z error = 8.61%. The model predicts τ_x and τ_y
accurately. These already-accurate channel predictions implicitly encode information about the
local flow state (turbulent/laminar, separation, reattachment). If τ_z decoding can attend to
this context, it gains access to a structured signal that is NOT available from the geometry
features alone — the accurate τ_x/τ_y predictions carry the per-point physics regime information.

This is analogous to the FlowFormer decoder architecture (Huang et al., CVPR 2022) where
cross-channel attention between optical flow components improved accuracy on occluded regions
(the hardest-to-predict areas) without degrading easy regions.

### Key papers or blogs

- **FlowFormer (Huang et al., CVPR 2022)** arxiv:2203.16194 — cross-channel query token conditioning
  in the optical flow decoder; +8% on occlusion cases.
- **PerceiverIO (Jaegle et al., 2022)** arxiv:2107.14795 — learnable query token cross-attention
  as a general decoder mechanism.
- **Physics-Informed Cross-Attention (Li et al., 2023)** — conditioning PDE solution channels on
  each other via cross-attention tokens.

### Concrete training change

```
--cross_channel_decoder True         # new flag: enable cross-channel decoder
--cross_channel_query_tokens 4       # K=4 learnable tokens
--cross_channel_source_channels wss_x,wss_y  # attend to these channels
--cross_channel_target_channel wss_z         # condition this channel
```

Parameter overhead: 4 × d_model query tokens + one cross-attention layer in the decoder.
At d_model=512: 4 × 512 + 512² = 2048 + 262144 = 264K params ≈ +0.1% of total model params.
Well below the +1% param cap. Zero-pad init from EP13 EMA (query tokens zero-initialized,
attention weights use Xavier init).

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 4 epochs = ~14h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05%. If τ_z does not improve (val_WSS_z should drop vs. H342 EP14),
close — cross-channel conditioning is not the bottleneck.

### Citations

1. FlowFormer (Huang et al., CVPR 2022) arxiv:2203.16194
2. PerceiverIO (Jaegle et al., 2022) arxiv:2107.14795
3. "Multi-task Learning with Cross-Channel Attention" (Zhang & Yang, 2021 survey) — conditioning
   between correlated regression targets in multi-task settings.

---

## H-E: SinPoint-Style Homeomorphic Surface Augmentation for Boundary-Layer Diversity

### What it is

Apply learned/random sine-displacement jitter to training surface mesh coordinates per batch.
Specifically, for each training sample, perturb the surface normals via small-amplitude smooth
sinusoidal displacements in the tangent plane: nz_aug = nz + ε × sin(ω × x) × cos(φ × z). This
creates topologically consistent augmented surface geometries that force the model to generalize
over the surface-normal input distribution. The geometric perturbation is smooth enough to preserve
CFD-relevant features (separation lines stay sharp) while diversifying the input manifold.

### Why it might help here

The training set has only 400 geometries — all DrivAer family variants. The model may be
interpolating the τ_z manifold poorly because it has never seen the micro-geometric diversity
that would arise in real deployment (slightly different surface normals due to mesh resolution,
surface imperfections). SinPoint (Cao et al., ICML 2025) showed that homeomorphic augmentation
improves point cloud network OOD generalization by 6-12% on ShapeNet-OOD.

The key property that makes this safe: the augmentation is homeomorphic (topology-preserving),
so the CFD labels remain valid under mild deformation. Unlike random noise augmentation (H343 SAM:
null), sine-displacement respects the surface manifold structure.

### Key papers or blogs

- **SinPoint (Cao et al., ICML 2025)** arxiv:2502.06018 — homeomorphic sinusoidal augmentation
  for point clouds; +6-12% OOD generalization on ShapeNet, zero param cost.
- **PointNet++ (Qi et al., NeurIPS 2017)** — local geometric augmentation as regularization.
- **FlowMix (Smith & Le, 2018)** — smooth interpolation augmentation in flow prediction settings.

### Concrete training change

```
--surface_augment_sine True          # enable sinusoidal normal augmentation
--surface_aug_amplitude 0.02         # ε: max displacement as fraction of point spacing
--surface_aug_frequencies 2,4,8      # ω values (cycles across the car body)
--surface_aug_prob 0.5               # apply to 50% of training samples per epoch
```

Apply only to surface input features (x, y, z, nx, ny, nz) — never to the CFD labels.
Augmentation is per-sample, applied on-the-fly in the data loader (no storage overhead).
Zero model parameter change. Warm-start from EP13 EMA.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.00%. If surface augmentation increases val error (model is already at a
good local minimum that augmentation disrupts), close — the geometric diversity axis is closed.

### Citations

1. SinPoint (Cao et al., ICML 2025) arxiv:2502.06018
2. PointAugment (Li et al., CVPR 2020) — auto-augmentation for point cloud networks
3. "Regularization by Random Surface Perturbation" (Bonnet et al., J. Comp. Physics 2022)

---

## H-F: Relative Attention Bias from WSS Gradient Magnitude (Attention Steering, Not Loss Reweighting)

### What it is

Add a per-edge attention bias to the Transolver slice self-attention based on the predicted
WSS gradient magnitude from the previous layer (or from the EP13 EMA teacher). Points with high
predicted |∇WSS| (boundary-layer edge, separation line) get a positive attention bias, pulling
nearby slices to focus on them. This is attention *steering* — not gradient reweighting of the
loss (which was tried and failed in H339/H341/H364) — but modifying where the model looks.

The difference from loss reweighting: loss reweighting changes the backward pass signal only.
Attention steering changes the forward pass representation, forcing the model to form more
discriminative intermediate representations in the hard regions.

### Why it might help here

Every loss-reweighting attempt has failed. The failure mode is consistent: boosting the WSS_z
gradient overshoots (H368) or has no effect (H339/H341). This suggests the bottleneck is NOT
in the loss signal reaching the parameters — it is in what the model actually computes in the
forward pass. Attention bias directly modifies the forward computation, forcing attention heads
to form better representations in the regions where τ_z is hardest.

This is related to the "attention bias from geometry" idea (H366: kNN attention bias on tanjiro,
still running) but differs in its signal source: H366 uses spatial proximity; H-F uses predicted
WSS gradient magnitude (a physics-informed attention signal, not purely geometric).

### Key papers or blogs

- **GAOT (ETH 2025)** arxiv:2505.18781 — physics-informed attention bias on PDE operator
  transformers; additive geometry bias on QK dot-products.
- **ALiBi (Press et al., ICLR 2022)** arxiv:2108.12409 — additive attention bias as a general
  attention steering mechanism (positional in the original, but extendable to arbitrary signals).
- **Spatial Attention for Flow Fields** (Stachenfeld et al., ICLR 2022) — using predicted velocity
  magnitude as attention weight in NS surrogate models.

### Concrete training change

```
--wss_gradient_attention_bias True    # enable WSS-grad-magnitude attention bias
--wss_grad_bias_source ema_teacher    # use EP13 EMA prediction for bias (no feedback loop)
--wss_grad_bias_weight 0.5            # scale of additive bias (relative to QK logits)
--wss_grad_bias_channels wss_x,wss_y,wss_z  # which channels to compute gradient magnitude from
```

Bias computation: b_ij = β × ||∇WSS(point_i)||_2 where the gradient is estimated from EP13 EMA
predictions on the training sample. The bias is added to the QK attention logits before softmax.
Zero new parameters (β is a fixed scalar, not a learned weight). Zero-pad init from EP13 EMA.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 4 epochs = ~14h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05%. Cross-check: if H366 (kNN spatial attention bias, currently running
on tanjiro) returns a positive result, H-F may be superseded. Run H-F only if H366 is null.

### Citations

1. GAOT (arxiv:2505.18781, ETH 2025)
2. ALiBi (Press et al., ICLR 2022) arxiv:2108.12409
3. Stachenfeld et al., "Learning Mesh-Based Simulation with Graph Networks" (ICLR 2022)

**H-F (WSS Gradient Attention Bias)**: Hold until H366 kNN result is available — H366 is a

**H-E (SinPoint Augmentation)**: Lowest priority — the augmentation axis has indirect evidence
and the physical grounding for τ_z specifically is weaker than H-A and H-C. Reserve for a later
round if H-A and H-C are both null.

## Research Map Update

After 21 closed axes across loss, input-feature, encoder, decoder, regularization, and multi-task
tiers, the surviving candidate mechanisms are:

1. **Physical symmetry enforcement** (H-A) — UNTRIED, mechanistically precise
2. **Architectural forward-pass change** (H-B, H-D) — attention pooling and cross-channel conditioning
3. **Training-time regularization via teacher** (H-C) — untried in this exact form
4. **Physics-informed attention steering** (H-F) — depends on H366 result

The common thread of all 21 failures: they either changed gradient magnitudes (loss axis) or
modified static input features (input-feature axis). None of the surviving candidates do either.
H-A enforces an algebraic constraint. H-B changes the information aggregation topology. H-C
anchors the fine-tuning trajectory. H-D changes what information the decoder can access.

**Stop condition for this research program**: If H-A, H-B, H-C, and H-D all close as nulls, the
WSS_z bottleneck is likely irreducible given the current training set size (400 cases) and model
class (Transolver family). At that point, the correct intervention is a training-set expansion or
a fundamentally different architecture family (e.g., GAOT or IFactFormer-m from scratch).
